enrichment.short_description: Airbrake is an error and performance monitoring platform that detects, groups, and pinpoints application errors in real time.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Airbrake error monitoring: real-time error tracking, performance insights, and deployment tracking for web, mobile, and desktop apps.

features.title: Real-Time Error Monitoring
features.description: Catches application errors with line-of-code precision and groups them intelligently so teams can prioritize fixes.

features.title: Performance Monitoring
features.description: Lightweight APM tracks HTTP request response times and user satisfaction alongside error data.

features.title: Deployment Tracking
features.description: Correlates errors with deployments to help teams identify which release introduced a problem.

features.title: Multi-Language SDKs
features.description: Official libraries cover JavaScript, Python, Ruby, Java, Go, PHP, .NET, and other languages.

features.title: Single Sign-On
features.description: Supports SSO for centralized team access to Airbrake accounts.

features.title: Usage Caps
features.description: Teams can set spending limits to control costs and avoid unexpected overage charges.

pros: SDKs available for many popular languages and frameworks
Error and performance monitoring can be scaled independently by usage tier
Usage caps help prevent unexpected overage charges
Backed by LogicMonitor for stable ownership and infrastructure
4.7-star rating on G2 based on user reviews
cons: Error monitoring and performance monitoring are billed as separate line items
Entry-level Dev plan is limited to a single user
Full pricing depends on a tiered error-volume structure that requires reading the fine print

faqs.question: Does Airbrake offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Airbrake offers a free trial with access to all features, including unlimited errors and unlimited performance monitoring events.

faqs.question: How much does Airbrake cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at $19/month (Dev + Errors) billed monthly, or $17/month billed annually, with higher tiers at $38/month and $76/month.

faqs.question: Can I use Airbrake for both errors and performance?
faqs.answer: Yes, but error monitoring and performance monitoring are billed as separate add-ons.

faqs.question: What languages does Airbrake support?
faqs.answer: Airbrake provides SDKs for JavaScript, Python, Ruby, Java, Go, PHP, .NET, and other languages.

faqs.question: Who owns Airbrake?
faqs.answer: Airbrake is owned by LogicMonitor.

use_cases.title: Web application error tracking
use_cases.description: Development teams use Airbrake to catch and triage runtime errors across web apps before they affect end users.

use_cases.title: Mobile and desktop error monitoring
use_cases.description: Airbrake's SDKs let mobile and desktop app teams track crashes and exceptions in production.

use_cases.title: Release quality tracking
use_cases.description: DevOps teams correlate new deployments with error spikes to quickly identify and roll back problematic releases.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Airbrake?
paragraphs: Airbrake is an error monitoring and performance insights platform that catches application errors, groups them intelligently, and pinpoints the exact line of code responsible so developers can fix problems before customers are affected.

The platform also includes lightweight application performance monitoring and deployment tracking, letting teams correlate error spikes with specific releases. Airbrake is owned by LogicMonitor and reports being trusted by more than 1,600 organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Airbrake is for
paragraphs: Airbrake is built for developers and DevOps teams building web, mobile, and desktop applications who need real-time error detection with SDKs for languages including JavaScript, Python, Ruby, Java, Go, PHP, and .NET.
It suits teams that want error tracking and performance monitoring in one account, with usage caps to keep costs predictable as error volume grows.

enrichment.short_description: GlitchTip is an open-source, Sentry-compatible error and performance monitoring platform available as SaaS or self-hosted.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: GlitchTip: open-source error tracking and performance monitoring, self-hosted or hosted, compatible with Sentry SDKs.

features.title: Error Tracking
features.description: Captures exceptions, log messages, and CSP violations in real time.

features.title: Performance Monitoring
features.description: Flags slow web requests, database calls, and transactions.

features.title: Uptime Monitoring
features.description: Pings sites and sends alerts via email or webhook when they become unresponsive.

features.title: Log Search
features.description: Search and filter application logs alongside error and performance data.

features.title: Sentry SDK Compatibility
features.description: Works with Sentry's open-source SDKs, easing migration for existing Sentry users.

features.title: Self-Hosting Option
features.description: Fully open-source codebase that can be deployed and run on your own server.

pros: Open source with a self-hosted option for full data control
Free hosted tier covers up to 1,000 events per month
Compatible with existing Sentry SDKs, easing migration
Discounted pricing available for non-profits and open-source projects
EU hosting option in Germany for data sovereignty
cons: Higher-volume plans (Medium, Large) get costly at scale, up to $250/month
Priority support and BAA availability are limited to higher tiers
Self-hosting requires managing your own infrastructure

faqs.question: Is GlitchTip free?
faqs.answer: Yes, GlitchTip offers a free hosted tier for up to 1,000 events per month, and the software itself is fully open source for self-hosting at no cost.

faqs.question: Is GlitchTip compatible with Sentry?
faqs.answer: Yes, GlitchTip works with Sentry's open-source SDKs.

faqs.question: Does GlitchTip offer non-profit discounts?
faqs.answer: Yes, non-profit and open-source project discounts start at $5/month.

faqs.question: Can I self-host GlitchTip?
faqs.answer: Yes, GlitchTip can be deployed on your own server as a self-hosted instance.

faqs.question: Where is GlitchTip's EU hosting located?
faqs.answer: GlitchTip offers EU hosting in Germany for organizations with data sovereignty requirements.

use_cases.title: Error tracking for small teams
use_cases.description: Small teams use GlitchTip's free or Small plan to monitor errors without committing to expensive SaaS tools.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted monitoring for compliance
use_cases.description: Organizations with data residency or HIPAA requirements self-host GlitchTip or use its BAA-available Large plan.

use_cases.title: Sentry migration
use_cases.description: Teams already using Sentry SDKs can switch to GlitchTip without changing their instrumentation code.

features: Priority email and live chat support

long_form_blocks.heading: What is GlitchTip?
paragraphs: GlitchTip is an open-source error tracking platform that collects exceptions, log messages, and CSP violations in real time, organizes them, and alerts teams when and where they need to know.
Beyond error tracking, GlitchTip includes performance monitoring, uptime monitoring, and log search, and is compatible with Sentry's open-source SDKs. It was created by Burke Software and Consulting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who GlitchTip is for
paragraphs: GlitchTip suits individual developers, small teams, and growing organizations that want error tracking without the cost of larger commercial SaaS platforms.
Its self-hosted, open-source option also appeals to teams with data residency or compliance requirements, including EU hosting and BAA availability on higher tiers.

enrichment.short_description: Errbit is a free, open-source, self-hosted error catcher that is API-compatible with Airbrake.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Errbit: open-source, self-hosted error tracking tool that's API-compatible with Airbrake, built in Ruby.

features.title: Self-Hosted Deployment
features.description: Runs on your own infrastructure, giving full control over error data.

features.title: Airbrake API Compliance
features.description: Existing Airbrake clients can point their airbrake gem or SDK at an Errbit instance with no code changes.

features.title: Open Source (MIT)
features.description: Free to use, modify, and deploy under the MIT license.

features.title: Docker Support
features.description: Available as a Docker image for simplified deployment.

features.title: Ruby/MongoDB Stack
features.description: Built on Ruby and MongoDB as the underlying data store.

pros: Completely free and open source under the MIT license
Drop-in replacement for Airbrake via API compatibility
Full control over error data since it's self-hosted
Active community with thousands of GitHub stars and ongoing releases
Available as a Docker image for easier deployment
cons: Requires self-hosting and infrastructure maintenance with no managed cloud option
Requires specific Ruby and MongoDB versions
No official vendor support or SLA
Lacks the performance monitoring/APM features of paid alternatives like Airbrake

faqs.question: Is Errbit free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Errbit is free, open-source software licensed under MIT.

faqs.question: Can I migrate from Airbrake to Errbit?
faqs.answer: Yes, Errbit is Airbrake API compliant, so you can redirect your existing Airbrake gem or SDK to your Errbit instance.

faqs.question: How is Errbit deployed?
faqs.answer: Errbit can be deployed from source or as a Docker image, and requires Ruby and MongoDB.

faqs.question: Is Errbit actively maintained?
faqs.answer: Yes, Errbit has an active commit history and regular releases on GitHub.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted error tracking
use_cases.description: Teams that need full control over their error data self-host Errbit instead of using a SaaS error tracker.

use_cases.title: Airbrake migration
use_cases.description: Organizations already using Airbrake SDKs can switch to Errbit without changing their instrumentation code.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Errbit?
paragraphs: Errbit is an open source, self-hosted error catcher that collects and aggregates errors from applications. It is Airbrake API compliant, meaning teams can redirect their existing Airbrake integration to an Errbit instance without changing code.
The project is MIT-licensed, built on Ruby and MongoDB, and distributed both as source code on GitHub and as a Docker image for simplified deployment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Errbit is for
paragraphs: Errbit is for engineering teams that want an Airbrake-compatible error tracker but need to keep error data on their own infrastructure rather than a third-party SaaS service.
It fits teams comfortable running and maintaining a self-hosted Ruby and MongoDB application in exchange for a free, fully open-source error tracking tool.

enrichment.short_description: OpenObserve is an open-source observability platform unifying logs, metrics, traces, and RUM with low-cost object storage.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenObserve: open-source observability platform for logs, metrics, traces, and RUM with low-cost storage and SQL/PromQL queries.

features.title: Unified Observability
features.description: Combines logs, metrics, traces, dashboards, alerts, and real user monitoring in one platform.

features.title: Low-Cost Object Storage
features.description: Uses Apache Parquet columnar compression on object storage to cut storage costs versus systems like Elasticsearch.

features.title: SQL and PromQL Queries
features.description: Query telemetry data using familiar SQL and PromQL syntax.

features.title: OpenTelemetry Native
features.description: Ingests data via OpenTelemetry with no proprietary vendor lock-in.

features.title: Single Binary Deployment
features.description: Runs as a single binary or via Helm chart on Kubernetes.

features.title: AI Assistant and SRE Agent
features.description: Natural-language queries and automated root-cause analysis available on Cloud/Enterprise plans.

pros: Free, fully open-source OSS edition with no usage limits
Self-hosted Enterprise tier is free up to 50 GB/day ingestion
Lower storage costs than Elasticsearch via columnar compression
14-day free trial on Cloud plans with no credit card required
SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001 certified on the Cloud offering
cons: Cloud pricing is usage-based per GB, which can be harder to predict at scale
Advanced AI and enterprise capabilities require a paid or Enterprise plan
Self-hosted Enterprise features beyond 50 GB/day require contacting sales

faqs.question: Is OpenObserve free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the self-hosted OSS edition is free with unlimited usage, and Cloud plans include a 14-day free trial.

faqs.question: What data types does OpenObserve support?
faqs.answer: Logs, metrics, traces, RUM, and session replay in one unified platform.

faqs.question: How is OpenObserve priced in the cloud?
faqs.answer: Cloud plans charge $0.50/GB for ingestion and $0.01/GB for queries, with a 30% discount for annual commitment.

faqs.question: Can I self-host OpenObserve for free at scale?
faqs.answer: Self-hosted Enterprise is free up to 50 GB/day of ingestion; above that requires contacting sales.

faqs.question: Is OpenObserve open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's licensed under AGPL-3.0 with more than 20,000 GitHub stars.

use_cases.title: Unified log and metrics platform
use_cases.description: Teams replace separate logging, metrics, and tracing tools with OpenObserve's single unified platform.

use_cases.title: Cost-sensitive observability at scale
use_cases.description: Organizations ingesting large volumes of telemetry use OpenObserve's object-storage architecture to reduce storage costs versus Elasticsearch.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted compliance deployments
use_cases.description: Teams with data residency needs run OpenObserve self-hosted rather than sending data to a third-party cloud.

features: Logs, metrics, traces, RUM, session replay

long_form_blocks.heading: What is OpenObserve?
paragraphs: OpenObserve is an open-source observability platform that unifies logs, metrics, traces, and real user monitoring in one deployment. It stores data on low-cost object storage using Apache Parquet columnar compression and supports SQL and PromQL for queries.
It is built for OpenTelemetry-native ingestion and can be deployed as a single binary or via a Helm chart on Kubernetes, with both a free open-source edition and paid Cloud and Enterprise tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who OpenObserve is for
paragraphs: OpenObserve is aimed at DevOps and platform engineering teams that want full-stack observability without running and paying for multiple separate tools.
Its low-cost storage architecture makes it a fit for teams managing large telemetry volumes who want to control storage spend compared to systems like Elasticsearch.

enrichment.short_description: Uptrace is an OpenTelemetry-based observability platform unifying traces, metrics, and logs on a ClickHouse backend.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Uptrace: open-source OpenTelemetry observability platform for traces, metrics, and logs on ClickHouse, self-hosted or cloud.

features.title: Service Graph & APM
features.description: Visualizes service dependencies and RED metrics (request rate, error rate, duration).

features.title: Zero-Code Instrumentation
features.description: Auto-instruments applications across 11+ programming languages.

features.title: OTel Arrow Compression
features.description: Uses the OTel Arrow protocol, claimed to deliver 15-30x compression over raw telemetry data.

features.title: SSO Support
features.description: SAML/OIDC single sign-on for enterprise identity providers.

features.title: Flexible Deployment
features.description: Available as Cloud, self-hosted, Kubernetes, Ansible, or Docker deployments.

features.title: AI Assistant Integration
features.description: Query telemetry data via MCP-compatible AI assistants such as Claude Code, Cursor, and GitHub Copilot.

pros: Free, unlimited self-hosted Community Edition available on GitHub
Cloud free tier includes 50 GB storage and 5,000 timeseries per month with no card required
Transparent per-GB pricing with no seat or host-count charges
Auto-instrumentation across 11+ languages
99.9% uptime SLA on the Cloud offering
cons: Cold storage and extended data retention incur additional per-GB charges
Licensed on-premises/enterprise tier requires custom pricing
Smaller community/ecosystem than larger established observability vendors

faqs.question: Is Uptrace free?
faqs.answer: The self-hosted Community Edition is free and unlimited; the Cloud plan includes a free tier of 50 GB storage and 5,000 timeseries per month.

faqs.question: How is Uptrace Cloud priced?
faqs.answer: Pay-per-GB for traces and logs starting at $0.075/GB, and per-datapoint for metrics starting at $0.025 per 1 million datapoints, with volume discounts.

faqs.question: Does Uptrace support OpenTelemetry?
faqs.answer: Yes, Uptrace is built around OpenTelemetry data and also accepts Prometheus, Vector, FluentBit, and CloudWatch data.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Uptrace?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Community Edition is open source and free to self-host; a licensed on-premises option is also available.

faqs.question: Does Uptrace integrate with AI coding assistants?
faqs.answer: Yes, via MCP it can be queried from AI assistants such as Claude Code, Cursor, and GitHub Copilot.

use_cases.title: Distributed tracing for microservices
use_cases.description: Engineering teams use Uptrace's service graph and APM to trace requests and monitor RED metrics across distributed systems.

use_cases.title: Cost-conscious observability
use_cases.description: Teams switching from per-seat observability vendors adopt Uptrace's per-GB pricing to reduce costs.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted telemetry
use_cases.description: Organizations that must keep telemetry data in-house run the free Community Edition on their own infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Uptrace?
paragraphs: Uptrace is an observability platform that unifies application tracing, metrics, and logs in a single interface, processing telemetry using OpenTelemetry standards on a ClickHouse-based backend.
It offers a service graph, zero-code auto-instrumentation across 11+ languages, and flexible deployment as a managed Cloud service, self-hosted Community Edition, or licensed on-premises install.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Uptrace is for
paragraphs: Uptrace is built for engineering teams, SREs, and DevOps professionals managing distributed systems who want OpenTelemetry-based observability without vendor lock-in.
Its transparent per-GB pricing and free self-hosted Community Edition also appeal to teams looking to control observability costs compared to seat- or host-based vendors.

enrichment.short_description: HyperDX is an open-source observability platform unifying session replay, logs, traces, metrics, and errors, powered by ClickHouse.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: HyperDX: open-source observability platform for logs, traces, metrics, session replay, and errors, built on ClickHouse.

features.title: End-to-End Tracing
features.description: Automatically correlates requests from browsers or mobile apps through backend services.

features.title: ClickHouse-Powered Search
features.description: Searches terabytes of events in seconds.

features.title: Correlated Session Replay
features.description: Links user session recordings with backend logs and traces.

features.title: Automatic Pattern Clustering
features.description: Condenses billions of log events into recognizable patterns.

features.title: Chart Builder
features.description: Visualizes logs, metrics, and traces without writing complex queries.

features.title: OpenTelemetry Support
features.description: Vendor-agnostic instrumentation for logs, metrics, and traces.

features.title: Alert Integration
features.description: Sends notifications via Slack, email, or PagerDuty.

pros: Open source with an active GitHub community
Free tier available with no credit card required
No per-seat or per-host charges; pricing is based on storage volume only
Backed by Y Combinator and later acquired by ClickHouse, adding stability and resources
SOC 2 Type II certified
cons: Free tier is limited to 3 GB/month and 3-day data retention
Higher usage incurs per-GB overage charges ($0.40/GB)
Enterprise features like SAML SSO require a custom-priced plan

faqs.question: Is HyperDX free?
faqs.answer: Yes, HyperDX has a free tier (3 GB/month, 3-day retention, up to 1 user) with no credit card required.

faqs.question: How much does HyperDX cost?
faqs.answer: The Starter plan is $20/month for 50 GB of storage and 30-day retention with unlimited users; Enterprise is custom priced.

faqs.question: Is HyperDX open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, HyperDX is open source and available on GitHub.

faqs.question: Who owns HyperDX?
faqs.answer: HyperDX was built by DeploySentinel, Inc., a Y Combinator-backed startup, and was later acquired by ClickHouse.

faqs.question: What platforms does HyperDX support?
faqs.answer: It supports Node.js, Python, Java, Golang, Elixir, React Native, and browser JavaScript, deployable on Kubernetes, AWS EC2, Vercel, Fly.io, Heroku, and Cloudflare.

use_cases.title: End-to-end request debugging
use_cases.description: Engineering teams trace requests from the browser through backend services to debug production issues faster.

use_cases.title: Session replay with backend context
use_cases.description: Support and engineering teams replay user sessions alongside correlated logs and traces to diagnose bug reports.

use_cases.title: Cost-effective observability at scale
use_cases.description: Teams moving off per-seat tools use HyperDX's volume-based pricing to reduce observability costs.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is HyperDX?
paragraphs: HyperDX is an open-source observability platform that unifies session replay, logs, traces, metrics, and errors in one interface, letting teams trace requests end-to-end from a user's browser through backend servers automatically.
It is powered by ClickHouse for fast search across large event volumes and was built by Y Combinator-backed DeploySentinel, Inc. before being acquired by ClickHouse.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who HyperDX is for
paragraphs: HyperDX is for engineering teams that need affordable production debugging and monitoring without enterprise per-seat pricing.
Its free tier and volume-based pricing suit smaller teams, while unlimited users on the Starter plan and custom Enterprise options support larger organizations.

name: Logtail
skip_reason: Logtail has been rebranded to Better Stack Logs. The official site (betterstack.com/logs) no longer references the Logtail name anywhere, so enriching content under the Logtail brand would misrepresent the current product.

enrichment.short_description: CrowdSec is a crowd-sourced, open-source threat intelligence platform that blocks malicious IPs using shared attack data.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CrowdSec: open-source, crowd-sourced threat intelligence platform that blocks malicious IPs using shared attack data from a global network.

features.title: Community Blocklist
features.description: Crowd-sourced IP reputation data shared across a network of more than 70,000 users in 190+ countries.

features.title: Preemptive Blocking
features.description: Aims to block mass exploitation attempts before they reach protected servers.

features.title: Real-Time Signal Sharing
features.description: Aggregates around 15 million daily signals with roughly 50% daily IP rotation.

features.title: Plug-and-Play Integrations
features.description: Connects with common firewalls and CDNs.

features.title: Premium Blocklists
features.description: Curated blocklists by industry, tech stack, or country available on the Platinum tier.

features.title: IP Reputation API
features.description: On-demand IP reputation lookups with contextual threat data and MITRE classifications.

pros: Free, open-source Community Plan with core behavioral detection
Large crowd-sourced network of 70,000+ users across 190+ countries feeding threat data
Claims up to 95% reduction in mass exploitation attempts reaching protected systems
Plug-and-play integration with existing firewalls and CDNs
Multiple pricing tiers let teams pay only for the threat intelligence depth they need
cons: Advanced blocklists and CTI features require paid tiers priced from $1,900+/month for Platinum Blocklists
Full Console Premium pricing is not publicly listed and requires inquiry
Premium support and emergency bug fixes are separately priced add-ons

faqs.question: Is CrowdSec free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Community Plan is free and includes behavioral detection and automated response using the open-source Security Engine.

faqs.question: How much does CrowdSec's paid tier cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing varies by product; the IP Reputation API starts at $49/month for 5,000 queries, while Platinum Blocklists start around $1,900/month.

faqs.question: Is CrowdSec open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, the CrowdSec Security Engine is open-source software available on GitHub.

faqs.question: How large is CrowdSec's threat data network?
faqs.answer: More than 70,000 active users across 190+ countries share roughly 15 million signals daily.

faqs.question: Can I cancel a CrowdSec paid plan anytime?
faqs.answer: Yes, plans can be canceled at any time, effective at the end of the billing month.

use_cases.title: Preemptive IP blocking for servers
use_cases.description: Organizations deploy CrowdSec's Security Engine to block known malicious IPs before attacks reach their infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Reducing alert fatigue
use_cases.description: Security operations teams use CrowdSec's curated blocklists to cut down on the volume of low-value security alerts.

use_cases.title: Enterprise threat intelligence
use_cases.description: Larger organizations subscribe to Platinum Blocklists or the IP Reputation API for deeper, industry-specific threat context.

features: Curated blocklists by industry, tech stack, or country

long_form_blocks.heading: What is CrowdSec?
paragraphs: CrowdSec is a threat intelligence platform that uses crowd-sourced, AI-driven data and blocklists to help organizations preemptively block malicious IP addresses before attacks reach their servers.
Its open-source Security Engine detects and shares attack signals across a global network of users, and paid tiers add premium blocklists, an IP Reputation API, and enterprise console features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who CrowdSec is for
paragraphs: CrowdSec is for security operations teams and organizations that want to reduce alert fatigue and block distributed, mass-exploitation attacks using shared threat intelligence.
Its free Community Plan suits individuals and small teams, while Platinum Blocklists and the IP Reputation API target enterprises needing deeper, industry-specific threat context.

enrichment.short_description: Wazuh is a free, open-source XDR and SIEM platform for endpoint security, threat detection, and compliance monitoring.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Wazuh: free, open-source XDR and SIEM platform for endpoint security, threat detection, and cloud workload protection.

features.title: Endpoint Security
features.description: Configuration assessment, malware detection, and file integrity monitoring.

features.title: Threat Intelligence
features.description: Threat hunting, log data analysis, and vulnerability detection.

features.title: Active XDR
features.description: Real-time correlation with granular, on-device active responses.

features.title: Cloud Security
features.description: Container security and cloud workload posture management.

features.title: SIEM Capabilities
features.description: Centralized monitoring, alerting, and regulatory compliance support.

features.title: Open Source
features.description: Fully open source with ongoing community contributions on GitHub.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing costs
Large-scale adoption: 15+ million protected endpoints and 100,000+ enterprise users reported
Combines SIEM and XDR capabilities in a single platform
Works across public cloud, private cloud, and on-premises environments
Optional managed Wazuh Cloud and professional support/training available
cons: Self-managed deployments require in-house expertise to configure and maintain
Wazuh Cloud and professional services pricing is not publicly listed
Support relies partly on community channels unless a paid support contract is purchased

faqs.question: Is Wazuh free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Wazuh is free and open source with no license costs; paid Wazuh Cloud and support services are optional.

faqs.question: What is Wazuh used for?
faqs.answer: Wazuh provides SIEM and XDR capabilities including endpoint security, threat detection, vulnerability detection, and compliance monitoring.

faqs.question: Does Wazuh support cloud environments?
faqs.answer: Yes, it monitors public cloud, private cloud, and on-premises workloads, including container security.

faqs.question: How many organizations use Wazuh?
faqs.answer: Wazuh reports 15+ million protected endpoints and 100,000+ enterprise users.

faqs.question: Does Wazuh integrate with other security tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, including VirusTotal, TheHive, PagerDuty, and other third-party APIs.

use_cases.title: Unified SIEM and XDR
use_cases.description: Security teams use Wazuh to centralize log analysis, threat detection, and incident response across endpoints and cloud workloads.

use_cases.title: Regulatory compliance monitoring
use_cases.description: Organizations use Wazuh's IT hygiene and compliance modules to support regulatory requirements without commercial SIEM licensing costs.

use_cases.title: Cloud workload protection
use_cases.description: DevSecOps teams deploy Wazuh's cloud security modules for container security and posture management.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Wazuh?
paragraphs: Wazuh is a unified security platform providing Extended Detection and Response (XDR) and Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) capabilities. It monitors, detects, and alerts on security events across endpoints and cloud workloads.
The platform is free and fully open source, with paid options limited to managed Wazuh Cloud hosting, professional support, consulting, and training.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Wazuh is for
paragraphs: Wazuh is for organizations of any size that want scalable security monitoring without vendor lock-in or commercial SIEM licensing costs, from startups to large enterprises.
It fits teams needing endpoint security, threat hunting, vulnerability detection, and cloud workload protection in one open-source platform.

enrichment.short_description: Security Onion is a free, open-source platform for threat hunting, network security monitoring, and log management.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Security Onion: free, open-source platform for threat hunting, network security monitoring, and log management using Suricata, Zeek, and Elastic.

features.title: Network Security Monitoring
features.description: Built on Suricata, Zeek, and the Elastic Stack for full network visibility.

features.title: Threat Hunting
features.description: Tools for investigating and pivoting through security alerts and logs.

features.title: Log Management
features.description: Centralized collection and analysis of security logs.

features.title: Onion AI
features.description: AI-assisted alert analysis and detection tuning, with local model support available in the Pro version.

features.title: Community Detection Rules
features.description: Continuously updated open-source detection rulesets.

pros: Free and open source with no licensing cost for the core platform
Combines established tools (Suricata, Zeek, Elastic Stack) into one integrated platform
Long track record: created in 2008, with over 2 million downloads by 2021
Used across government, defense, Fortune 500, and academic sectors
Optional Pro tier adds AI-driven detection tuning and local model support
cons: Security Onion Pro pricing is not publicly disclosed
Requires in-house expertise to deploy and tune for network security monitoring
Onion AI Assistant and local model support require the paid Pro tier

faqs.question: Is Security Onion free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Security Onion is a free, open-source platform; a paid Pro tier with additional AI-driven features launched in 2024.

faqs.question: What tools does Security Onion integrate?
faqs.answer: It integrates Suricata, Zeek, and the Elastic Stack for network security monitoring and log management.

faqs.question: Who created Security Onion?
faqs.answer: Doug Burks founded Security Onion in 2008; Security Onion Solutions, LLC was established in 2014.

faqs.question: What is Onion AI?
faqs.answer: Onion AI is an AI-assisted feature for alert analysis and detection tuning, with local model support available in the Pro version.

faqs.question: Who uses Security Onion?
faqs.answer: It's used by federal, state, and municipal governments, the Department of Defense, Fortune 500 companies, universities, and financial institutions.

use_cases.title: Network security monitoring
use_cases.description: Security teams deploy Security Onion to monitor network traffic using Suricata and Zeek for intrusion detection.

use_cases.title: Threat hunting
use_cases.description: Analysts use Security Onion's integrated tools to hunt for and investigate security threats across logs and network data.

use_cases.title: Government and defense security monitoring
use_cases.description: Federal, state, and defense organizations use Security Onion for compliant, cost-effective network monitoring.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Security Onion?
paragraphs: Security Onion is a free and open platform for threat hunting, network security monitoring, and log management, integrating established open-source tools including Suricata, Zeek, and the Elastic Stack.

Doug Burks founded Security Onion in 2008, and Security Onion Solutions, LLC was established in 2014. Security Onion 2 launched in 2020, and the project passed 2 million downloads by 2021, with the Onion AI Assistant introduced in 2025.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Security Onion is for
paragraphs: Security Onion serves federal, state, and municipal governments, the Department of Defense, Fortune 500 enterprises, universities, financial institutions, utilities, and manufacturing organizations.
It fits teams that need free, open-source network security monitoring and threat hunting, with an optional paid Pro tier for organizations wanting AI-driven detection tuning.
